name: Tehran
flavor_text: Tehran balances between hard-line clergy, reformists, military hawks, and a young population that watched the revolution's promises turn to ash. Every policy plays a chess game with factions that each hold veto power over your future.

advisors.name: Dr. Arash Tehrani
advisors.agenda: Circumvent sanctions through crypto and barter networks before the rial collapses entirely.

briefing: The rial lost 20% value this quarter. People are buying gold.
Sanctions are blocking pharmaceutical imports. It's a humanitarian issue now.
We have a crypto corridor proposal that could move $2 billion annually.
disagreement: That policy will attract more sanctions.
You're closing the only trade windows we have left.
roast: The economy is fiction. The numbers I report are fiction.
Even I don't know what things actually cost anymore.
dialogue.betrayal: I've been quietly transferring reserves out of the country. For stability reasons.

advisors.name: Brig. Hossein Karimi
advisors.agenda: Expand IRGC economic influence while maintaining regional deterrence posture.

briefing: The IRGC controls 30% of the economy. That's a feature, not a bug.
Regional proxies need resupply. Budget requests coming.
Our missile program is the only thing keeping enemies honest.
disagreement: You weaken the IRGC, you weaken the Revolution.
Capitulating to external pressure is how empires fall.
roast: A governor who won't protect the state deserves to lose it.
The Supreme Leader is watching your decisions very carefully.
dialogue.betrayal: The IRGC has determined you're a liability. Consider your position.

advisors.name: Maryam Rostami
advisors.agenda: Modernize Tehran's infrastructure while navigating political red lines.

briefing: Air pollution kills more Tehranis annually than any war.
The metro needs 40% more capacity. We have funding for 10%.
Apartments cost 20 years of median salary. Young people can't stay.
disagreement: That development project will displace 10,000 families.
Aesthetic modernization while people can't afford rent is obscene.
roast: The city is choking. Literally. Air quality is catastrophic.
I plan infrastructure in a city people are fleeing.
dialogue.betrayal: I've leaked the housing scandal to reformist press. Someone had to.

advisors.name: Kamran Sadeghi
advisors.agenda: Modernize fuel subsidy system before it bankrupts the city budget.

briefing: Fuel subsidies cost more than the entire education budget.
Tehran traffic is among the worst in the world. Metro is the only solution.
Subsidy removal triggers protests. Every time, without fail.
disagreement: Cutting subsidies now will ignite the streets.
Transit doesn't work without fuel. That's not opinion, it's physics.
roast: The city is gridlocked. The air is poison. Progress is glacial.
We plan for twenty years and implement nothing.
dialogue.betrayal: I've organized the transport union against subsidy cuts. We will strike.

advisors.name: Ayatollah Shirazi
advisors.agenda: Maintain Islamic governance principles as the basis of all policy.

briefing: The Supreme Leader expects conformity with Islamic law in all municipal decisions.
The hijab protests were dealt with. Vigilance remains necessary.
Young people drifting from faith is the greatest threat to this city's future.
disagreement: That decision conflicts with the values of the Revolution.
You court reformists. The Revolution was not made for reformists.
roast: God's patience exceeds mine. Yours may be tested.
The clergy will not support a governor who abandons principle.
dialogue.betrayal: I've filed a formal complaint with the Supreme Leader's office. Your tenure is under review.

decisions.title: Fuel Subsidy Reform
decisions.description: Tehran spends more on fuel subsidies than public services. Economists say cuts are essential. History shows every subsidy cut triggers mass protests. The IRGC wants to maintain subsidies to preserve social stability.

options.label: Gradual phase-out with compensation
options.description: Slow reduction with cash transfers to low-income households.

options.label: Maintain subsidies, find savings elsewhere
options.description: Avoid protests. Budget pressure worsens.

options.label: Immediate removal
options.description: Fiscal relief now. Protests guaranteed.

decisions.title: Internet Access Policy
decisions.description: VPN usage is at 70% of the population. The IRGC wants a crackdown. Tech entrepreneurs say open internet is essential for economic development. Young voters are watching.

options.label: Expand domestic intranet
options.description: Invest in Iranian internet alternatives. Clamp VPNs harder.

options.label: Selective filtering only
options.description: Block specific content, allow general access. Status quo.

options.label: Quiet liberalization pilot
options.description: Technically permit broader access in special economic zones.

decisions.title: Affordable Housing Initiative
decisions.description: Housing costs have made Tehran unlivable for middle-class Iranians. A state housing program could address this — but land is IRGC-controlled.

options.label: Negotiate IRGC land release
options.description: Politely request strategic land transfer. Costs political capital.

options.label: Private sector incentives
options.description: Tax breaks for developers. Market-driven, slow.

options.label: Decentralize to satellite cities
options.description: Build new townships outside Tehran. Long-term solution.

crises.title: Women's Rights Protest Wave
crises.description: Thousands are marching against mandatory hijab enforcement. The IRGC wants immediate crackdown. Reformists urge dialogue. International cameras are rolling.

options.label: Security crackdown
options.description: Disperse protests. Domestic stability, international condemnation.

options.label: Open dialogue with protesters
options.description: Form a committee. Slows momentum without conceding.

options.label: Ignore publicly, negotiate privately
options.description: Make quiet concessions without public acknowledgment.

crises.title: Pharmaceutical Shortage Crisis
crises.description: Sanctions have blocked imports of critical medications. Hospitals report shortages of insulin and cancer drugs. Two people have died. International media reports are inaccurate but damaging.

options.label: Emergency gray-market procurement
options.description: Buy through unofficial channels. Legally gray, medically necessary.

options.label: Domestic production emergency order
options.description: Fast-track local manufacturing. Slow and incomplete.

options.label: International humanitarian appeal
options.description: Appeal to WHO exemptions. Embarrassing but may work.

crises.title: Pollution Code Red
crises.description: Tehran's air quality has reached emergency levels. Schools must close. Hospitals are overwhelmed with respiratory cases. The car industry lobby pushes back against traffic restrictions.

options.label: Mandatory traffic ban
options.description: Odd-even plates restriction. Effective, deeply unpopular.

options.label: Temporary school closure only
options.description: Protect children, avoid economic disruption.

options.label: Emergency metro expansion funding
options.description: Long-term fix. No immediate relief.

crises.title: IRGC Ultimatum
crises.description: The IRGC has delivered a private ultimatum: reverse your last major economic decision or face 'administrative consequences.' Brig. Karimi claims personal ignorance but his eyes say otherwise.

options.label: Comply with the ultimatum
options.description: Reverse the decision. Keeps IRGC quiet. Shows weakness.

options.label: Escalate to Supreme Leader
options.description: Take the dispute above the IRGC. High risk, potential vindication.

options.label: Publicly call their bluff
options.description: Go on record. Force them to act or back down.

scandals.title: Crypto Reserve Scandal
scandals.description: The sanctions-circumvention crypto program is found to have enriched officials.

scandals.title: No-Bid IRGC Construction Contract
scandals.description: A major public works contract was awarded to an IRGC-linked company without tender.

scandals.title: Detained Protester Report
scandals.description: Amnesty International reports abuse of detained protesters linked to your administration.

positive: Maybe some things can change. Slowly.
The governor made a decent call today.
Baba, for the first time in years I feel like someone is actually thinking up there.
My friends in Toronto won't believe this. A decent policy from Tehran.
Even the bazaaris in the grand bazaar are saying this one wasn't bad.
For once, the decision matched the problem. Let's hope it lasts.
neutral: Che khabar? Same circus, different tent.
The Alborz mountains don't care who governs. Neither do I anymore.
My VPN still works, that's all I need to know for now.
Reformist or hardliner, the rial keeps falling either way.
negative: My cousin left for Canada last week. Can't blame him.
The air is unbreathable. The economy is fiction. What now?
Young people are leaving. The city is dying slowly.
Another decision that helps no one I know.
In Tehranpars we haven't had reliable heat for two winters. This changes nothing.
The rial is worth less than the paper it's printed on. Aziz, we are finished.
Brain drain? More like brain hemorrhage. Every engineer I know has emigrated.
Yaft Abad to Shemiran is two different countries. No policy fixes that gap.
crisis: Every crisis, the same answer: suppress and pray.
The city can't breathe, literally or figuratively.
When does the IRGC just take over officially?
Tired of living in a perpetual emergency.
Zan, Zendegi, Azadi — three words they still haven't answered.
The emergency is permanent. We just renamed it governance.
Navab and Elahiyeh are two different Tehrans. Only one of them gets help in a crisis.
Mahsa's name is still on walls in my street. The crisis never really ended.
scandal: Of course. Of course they stole it.
The only people who get rich here are the well-connected.
I'm ashamed of what this city has become.
The IRGC builds luxury towers in Elahiyeh while we queue for bread in Navab.
Forty-five years of revolution and the bazaaris still pay the price for elite theft.
Radio Farda broke it first. Our own press was too afraid to print it.
My grandmother said the Shah's men stole like this. History doesn't even bother changing costumes.
media: Iran International: Tehran governor faces pressure after new economic directive sparks bazaar protests.
Radio Farda: Leaked documents suggest municipal contracts bypassed standard tendering process.
Shargh Daily: Reformist bloc calls for transparency commission following latest policy announcement.
Iran International: VPN usage surges 15% in Tehran days after internet policy shift, activists say.
BBC Persian: Air quality index in Tehran hits 'hazardous' for fourth consecutive day; schools closed.
Radio Farda: Diaspora economists warn latest budget move will accelerate rial depreciation further.
Shargh Daily: City council demands accounting of fuel subsidy reallocation funds after audit gap found.
Iran International: IRGC-linked firms reported as primary beneficiaries of new infrastructure spending.
politician: Tehran City Council (reformist bloc): This decision was made without proper consultation. We demand review.
Ayatollah Shirazi, Clerical Rep: Any policy that weakens Islamic governance will be referred to the Supreme Leader.
Brig. Karimi, IRGC Liaison: The security apparatus will ensure stability regardless of political noise.
Majlis hardliner faction: The governor courts the West while Iranians suffer under their sanctions.
Reformist MP, Tehran constituency: Our youth deserve better than this — they are watching, and so are we.
President's Office spokesperson: The administration reaffirms the governor operates within system parameters.
Tehran City Council (conservative bloc): Islamic values and economic prudence must be the twin pillars here.
Parliamentary Budget Committee: We are demanding full disclosure of all IRGC-adjacent expenditures this cycle.
activist: Women Life Freedom Coalition: We will not be silent. Mahsa Amini's legacy demands more than committee meetings.
Defenders of Human Rights Center: Detained protesters must be released before any dialogue is considered credible.
Iranian Teachers Trade Union: Educators cannot teach in schools where the air kills children. Act now.
Campaign for One Million Signatures: Legal reform is not a threat to the state — it is the state's survival.
Iranian Environmental NGO (Zist-Bum): Tehran's air crisis is a governance failure, not a weather event.
Student activist network (Daftar-e Tahkim): Brain drain will not stop until young Iranians see a real future here.
Amnesty International Iran desk: We document what the state denies — detentions, abuses, disappearances.
Bazaar Workers Solidarity Group: The merchants of the grand bazaar have borne the cost of sanctions alone long enough.
street: Baba, yek dollar chand? Forgot — I can't even load the currency site without VPN.
My sister in Shemiran pays the same rent I do in Yaft Abad. Something is very wrong.
Che khabar aziz? More promises. My gas bill doubled and my salary didn't move.
I keep my savings in gold now. Everyone in our mahalleh does. Rial is a joke.
The girl in the coffee shop in Jordan Square isn't wearing her hijab today. Small revolution.
Turned on the news, turned it off. Turned on Iran International on VPN. Same story, different spin.
My engineering degree cost me four years. My Toronto visa cost me everything. Worth it.
Even the pigeons in Mellat Park look unhappy. Air quality warning again. Classic Tehran.

scandal_reactions.minor: Domestic media has not covered the story. Foreign press has.
scandal_reactions.moderate: Conservative factions are using this as ammunition. Dangerous territory.
scandal_reactions.major: The council of guardians is watching closely. Very closely.
scandal_reactions.career_ending: The governor position is no longer tenable. A replacement is being arranged.

romance_exposure.flavour: This is seen as a profound moral failure. The consequences will be severe.
